topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of aged paper and strong coffee hung heavy in the air of the study. I found it tucked beneath a stack of financial reports: a manuscript. My breath hitched. It was my handwriting, meticulously rendered across the first few pages, detailing the minutiae of our shared apartment, the way I hum when I’m concentrating, the exact shade of blue my eyes turn when I’m amused. A cold knot tightened in my stomach as I flipped further, recognizing descriptions of arguments we’d had over burnt toast. I stared at the words, my hands trembling so badly I had to grip the desk edge. Who was this person, this unseen author, who had chronicled my existence with such intimate detail? I slowly pushed the manuscript away, the sound of the paper rustling like dry leaves amplifying the sudden, profound silence in the room.

[story 2]

It started innocently enough—a misplaced notebook. I was tidying up the bookshelf when I saw it, bound in worn leather, filled with flowing script. Curiosity, a sudden, sharp prickle, drew me closer. The first chapter, I realized with a slow, sinking feeling, was a portrait of me, capturing a moment of quiet introspection I hadn't even consciously registered. My jaw went slack. I read a passage describing my peculiar habit of reorganizing spices alphabetically, and a flush crept up my neck. I backed away, my feet feeling strangely disconnected from the floor. I kept glancing toward the bedroom door, half-expecting my partner to materialize, perhaps with a misplaced pen and an explanation that would unravel the reality I was currently inhabiting.

[story 3]

I found the laptop open on the kitchen counter, the screen illuminating a document titled, simply, 'The Subject.' My heart started hammering against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm. I leaned in, my vision blurring slightly as I scanned the text. It wasn't just observations; it was narrative. A description of my anxieties before sleep, the specific way I posture when nervous, the cadence of my laughter. A wave of disorientation washed over me. I stood frozen, the lukewarm tea forgotten in my hand. Every nerve ending felt hyper-alert, straining to process the impossible truth laid bare on the screen. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my throat making the action difficult, and then I simply turned and walked out of the room, needing air that felt suddenly too thin.
